db_hits.text:
Experimental Science is an Analyst Term whose importance in this portal is chiefly historiographical. It is strongly associated with large modern syntheses, above all Lynn Thorndike's A History of Magic and Experimental Science, in which magic and experiment appear as intertwined attempts to understand and manipulate nature. Medieval actors did not possess a single stable category equivalent to the phrase, and modern historians use it cautiously because it can blur crucial differences among natural philosophy, practical testing, occult causality, and illicit arts.

Historical Usage

There is no exact medieval actor term corresponding to experimental science as one unified field. Medieval writers spoke instead of experimentum, experience, mathematical arts, natural philosophy, properties, causes, and specific arts or operations. Figures such as Roger Bacon, Albertus Magnus, al-Kindi, Michael Scot, and later scholastic compilers become relevant here because they wrote about disciplined inquiry into causes, effects, rays, celestial influence, or hidden structures of nature. Those inquiries often touch the same problems modern historians place near magic.

That overlap is historically important, but it should not be overstated. Medieval authors did not simply think "science and magic are the same." They argued over what counted as lawful inquiry, what relied on occult properties, what could be demonstrated from nature, and what risked illicit dependence on stars, spirits, or unknown powers. Experimental science is therefore most useful as a label for an unstable zone of inquiry rather than as a triumphalist anticipation of modern laboratory science.

Thorndike and the Historiography of Continuity

The phrase matters above all because of the historiographical problem it names. Thorndike's monumental history insisted that medieval and early modern inquiry could not be narrated cleanly as the gradual shedding of magic in favor of science. Instead, attempts to explain unusual effects, verify powers, and manipulate nature often developed in close conversation with astrology, image magic, occult properties, and practical experimenta. Later scholarship has revised Thorndike sharply, but it has not made the underlying problem disappear.

Modern historians now tend to avoid any simple continuity story from magic to science. Yet the category remains useful because it reminds us that medieval natural inquiry did not grow in sterile isolation from occult speculation. The archive itself is messier: recipes, observations, image theories, ray theory, mineral virtues, and astral causation coexist in ways that force historians to think relationally.

Natural Inquiry and Occult Causality

One reason the category remains productive is that it highlights how often medieval inquiry focused on effects that were real or reputed but not fully explicable within ordinary causal language. Hidden virtues of stones, the action of celestial rays, the efficacy of properly timed images, or the transformation of substances all invited analysis that was neither straightforwardly magical nor fully normalized as scholastic natural philosophy. Experimental science, as an analytical label, points to that pressure point where explanation seeks discipline without yet attaining consensus.

Roger Bacon is especially important in this respect because later historians repeatedly recruited him into narratives of experiment, technology, and anti-superstitious critique. Albertus Magnus matters because his discussions of nature, minerals, and hidden properties reveal how inquiry into unusual effects could be methodical without being disenchanted. The category therefore serves less to canonize modern heroes than to illuminate medieval arguments over reliable knowledge.

Scholarly Significance

The category names both a temptation and an insight. The temptation is to read medieval inquiry as proto-modern science steadily purifying itself of magical residue. The insight is that many medieval attempts to explain, test, or manipulate unusual effects did inhabit a shared zone with occult causality, astral influence, and hidden virtues. Thorndike exaggerated some continuities, but he also forced the field to confront the archive that makes such continuities thinkable.

Later scholars have refined the problem by insisting on stronger distinctions among genres, institutions, and explanatory registers. Nicolas Weill-Parot, David Pingree, and others have shown that one must separate astral image theory, mathematical speculation, scholastic natural philosophy, and practical recipe culture with more care than Thorndike sometimes did. Even so, the phrase remains valuable because it names the historiographical work required to keep those distinctions visible without pretending the domains never touched.

Transmission and Variant Forms

Because this is an analyst term, its transmission occurs mainly through modern scholarship rather than through medieval manuscript self-description. Yet it organizes a very real medieval archive: treatises on rays, minerals, celestial influence, experimenta, and natural powers; compilations where practical recipes and theoretical discussions coexist; and later scholarly narratives that sought to bind these materials together. The portal uses the category not to collapse distinctions, but to help the user see why those distinctions became historically urgent.

Its variant forms are especially visible in the way different scholars have populated the label. For some, it stresses observation and practical verification. For others, it names an archive where occult explanation and natural inquiry remain intertwined. The portal follows the latter, more cautious usage: experimental science is a historiographical guide to a mixed field, not a declaration that medieval magic was secretly modern science in disguise.

Related Concepts

Experimental science borders closely on occult properties, natural powers, and celestial influence, all of which concern partially explained efficacy within nature. It also intersects with learned magic where the classificatory problem becomes especially sharp, and with mathematical arts where quantification, observation, and prediction overlap with suspect forms of knowledge.

Literature

Kieckhefer, Richard. Magic in the Middle Ages. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2014.
Lindberg, David C. Studies on medieval science and natural philosophy.
Thorndike, Lynn. A History of Magic and Experimental Science. New York: Columbia University Press, 1923-1958.
Weill-Parot, Nicolas. Studies on the relation of occult science, mathematics, and scholastic classification.

db_hits.text:
Natural Powers is a Hybrid category for medieval attempts to explain unusual efficacy through created nature rather than through explicit demonic pact. The phrase is useful because it gathers together one of the most important intellectual moves in the history of magic: the effort to say that strange effects may still be natural. In MedievalMagicDB it is a core bridge between natural philosophy, medicine, astrology, and the learned occult sciences.

Historical Usage

Medieval discussions of natural powers appear in works on stones, herbs, stars, bodies, rays, and hidden properties. Such discussions do not eliminate suspicion, but they provide alternatives to demonological explanation. Figures such as Albertus Magnus, Roger Bacon, and authors associated with Arabic technical traditions all become important here, because they show that effects later treated as magical could be described within a broader account of nature's capacities.

Scholarly Significance

The category matters because it prevents historians from assuming that every unusual effect in medieval discourse was either dismissed or demonized. Natural powers give the archive a third zone: serious but incomplete explanation. Modern scholarship uses this category to map the boundary where occult properties, celestial influence, and practical operations become hardest to sort. It is therefore central to both the intellectual and historiographical architecture of the portal.

Transmission and Variant Forms

Natural powers are discussed across treatises, commentaries, compendia, and later histories of science and magic. The category has no single founding text. Instead it recurs as an explanatory disposition across multiple genres and languages. That diffuse transmission is itself historically meaningful, since it shows how widely shared the pressure to naturalize efficacy could be.

Related Concepts

Natural powers belong closely with occult properties and celestial influence, since both concern partially visible causal structures. They also intersect with experimental science in later historiography and with talismans where naturalized explanations become most contested.

Literature

Kieckhefer, Richard. Magic in the Middle Ages. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2014.
Newman, William R., and other historians of medieval natural philosophy on hidden causation.
Thorndike, Lynn. A History of Magic and Experimental Science. New York: Columbia University Press, 1923-1958.
Weill-Parot, Nicolas. Studies on astral images and natural explanation.
Page, Sophie, and Catherine Rider, eds. The Routledge History of Medieval Magic. London: Routledge, 2019.

db_hits.text:
Demonology is an Analyst Term for learned discourse about demons: their ontology, powers, limits, deceptive capacities, and relation to human ritual action. It is a crucial category for medieval magic scholarship because many condemnations of magic are really arguments about what demons can accomplish and how they may insinuate themselves into practices of divination, healing, image use, or spirit invocation. Yet demonology should never be allowed to stand in for the whole field of magic. It is one powerful framework among others.

Historical Usage

Medieval actors did not usually gather all relevant materials under a single neutral heading equivalent to the modern word demonology. Rather, discussions of demons appear in theology, biblical exegesis, pastoral literature, scholastic disputation, exorcistic practice, and polemic against suspect arts. This dispersed archive is historically important. It shows that demonological reasoning was not marginal but woven into mainstream religious thought. At the same time, the relation between demons and magic was not uniform. Some texts make demons central to efficacy; others argue that certain effects can be natural or illusory instead.

Scholarly Significance

Demonology matters because it helps historians understand how the category of illicit magic was built. William of Auvergne, Thomas Aquinas, Augustine, and later demonological writers all provided conceptual grammars for reading ritual actions through demonic causation. Modern scholars such as Michael D. Bailey and Richard Kieckhefer have shown that this framework must be studied alongside manuscript evidence rather than accepted as direct description of practice. Demonology is therefore both source and problem: it tells us how authorities thought, and it risks obscuring how practitioners understood themselves.

Transmission and Variant Forms

The field moves across patristic, scholastic, pastoral, and legal traditions. A demonological claim in Augustine does not function exactly like one in a confessor's manual, an exorcistic rite, or a later witchcraft treatise. The category also shifts across vernacularization and institutional use. Medieval demonology is thus better treated as a family of discourses than as a single stable science. The modern term names a real scholarly object, but a composite one.

Related Concepts

Demonology is tightly linked to exorcism, where demonic presence becomes a matter of ritual management. It also intersects with necromancy and demonic pact, though those concepts concern particular forms of suspected or theorized relation to spirits. In histories of accusation it also bears strongly on witchcraft and maleficium.

Literature

Bailey, Michael D. Studies on medieval demonology, religion, and witchcraft.
Kieckhefer, Richard. Magic in the Middle Ages. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2014.
Rider, Catherine. Studies on theology, pastoral care, and suspect practices.
Thorndike, Lynn. A History of Magic and Experimental Science. New York: Columbia University Press, 1923-1958.
Page, Sophie, and Catherine Rider, eds. The Routledge History of Medieval Magic. London: Routledge, 2019.
db_hits.extra: Demonology is an Analyst Term for systematic learned discussion of demons, their powers, limitations, deception, and relation to divination, magic, superstition, and witchcraft.

db_hits.text:
Witchcraft is an Analyst Term for a historically contingent cluster of accusations, learned constructions, beliefs, and legal or theological categories that became especially powerful in the later Middle Ages and early modern period. It does not map cleanly onto a single medieval actor term. Medieval writers more often used words such as maleficium, superstitio, sortilegium, and related labels of harm, false religion, or illicit practice. The portal therefore treats witchcraft as a necessary but unstable modern category whose value depends on careful attention to local evidence, chronology, and vocabulary.

Historical Usage

The medieval roots of what modern historians call witchcraft are dispersed across sermons, confessional manuals, miracle stories, canon law, scholastic theology, princely advice, vernacular narrative, and judicial record. A village-level charge of bodily or agricultural harm, a theologian's discussion of demons, and a pastoral warning against suspect cures all contribute different strands to the category. This dispersion matters because it means witchcraft was assembled historically out of partially connected fears and explanatory habits rather than inherited as a single fixed social reality.

Many early and central uses of the evidence revolve around harm. Livestock fail, weather threatens, fertility is disrupted, illness persists, impotence is feared, and someone is said to have caused it. Yet the explanatory language for such harm remains variable. One source may treat the problem as maleficium, another as deception by demons, another as superstitious misuse of sacred words or objects, and still another as social suspicion with minimal theoretical elaboration. The term "witchcraft" is therefore strongest when it is used not as an assumed essence but as a map of converging accusations and interpretations.

By the thirteenth through fifteenth centuries, several developments begin to tighten the field. Scholastic demonology expands the explanatory place of the devil and subordinate spirits. Pastoral programs sharpen concern with illicit rites and suspect consultations. Judicial and inquisitorial procedures provide stronger institutional mechanisms for gathering testimony and codifying suspicion. Vernacular didactic texts and learned polemics alike begin to align harmful action, hidden ritual, perverse intention, and demonic agency more explicitly than before. None of these developments alone "creates" witchcraft, but together they make the category more historically coherent and more socially dangerous.

Analyst Term and Actor Vocabulary

The category is methodologically important because it is one of the clearest places where modern analytical language can obscure historical vocabulary. Medieval sources usually do not say "witchcraft" in the modern historian's broad sense. They name injuries, unlawful observances, charms, prognostications, image rites, and demonic acts more specifically. To write the history of witchcraft well is therefore to preserve the distance between analyst shorthand and actor speech. The portal keeps that distance visible by linking witchcraft outward to narrower entries rather than letting it swallow them.

This actor-analyst tension also helps explain why the category must remain relational. A confessor concerned about a spoken charm, a theologian arguing about diabolic illusion, and a judge pursuing accusations of harmful magic are not simply describing one shared object. They are repositioning overlapping materials within different institutional frames. Witchcraft is the modern historian's name for that unstable but consequential overlap.

Scholarly Significance

Modern scholarship uses witchcraft because some larger term is needed for the convergence of harmful magic, demonological suspicion, social accusation, and legal or moral classification. Michael D. Bailey has been especially important in showing how this convergence belongs to the history of religion, classification, and pastoral reform rather than to a simple story of irrational panic. Richard Kieckhefer's work remains crucial because it helps keep clerical and learned forms of illicit ritual in view even where later witchcraft discourse tries to redraw all magical practice under a single demonological heading.

Catherine Rider, Sophie Page, and contributors to broader synthetic volumes have also helped integrate witchcraft back into the wider ecology of medieval magic. That move matters because the field can otherwise split artificially between elite ritual magic on one side and village accusation on the other. Medieval evidence rarely respects such a clean division. Theories, texts, and accusations moved across social registers in messy ways, and witchcraft is one of the places where that mess becomes historically visible.

Accusation, Practice, and Social Form

Witchcraft should not be reduced either to actual ritual practice or to fantasy imposed from above. Medieval evidence frequently contains both practice claims and accusatory projection, and the relative balance changes by genre. Some records preserve only polemical attribution. Others point to routines of healing, cursing, protective speech, or consultation that may have been locally meaningful apart from learned demonology. Historians therefore have to distinguish among performed action, narrated suspicion, legal scripting, and theological interpretation.

This distinction is especially important for the medieval period, where later early modern stereotypes can easily distort the archive. The sabbath complex, systematic confessions of pact, and strong gendered consolidation associated with later witch persecutions have medieval antecedents, but they should not be retrojected wholesale into earlier centuries. The portal's entry architecture accordingly treats witchcraft as a moving category with a long prehistory rather than as a fully formed medieval constant.

Transmission and Variant Forms

Witchcraft is shaped not by one grimoire or one legal code but by the circulation of ideas across theology, canon law, pastoral practice, miracle narratives, exempla, demonological speculation, and vernacular teaching. In some texts, harmful action is central and demonic theory secondary. In others, the reverse is true. Some sources foreground women; others worry more about clerics, diviners, healers, or household specialists. The category's variation is therefore part of its history, not an imperfection to be cleaned away.

The relation between witchcraft and learned magic is likewise unstable. Some authors carefully distinguish clerical ritual books from popular harmful practices. Others absorb both into a single logic of illicit commerce with spirits. This instability is one reason the portal treats witchcraft as a hub entry: it forces the user to browse among accusation, ritual technique, moral theology, and institutional classification rather than settling too quickly on one explanatory model.

Related Concepts

Witchcraft overlaps most obviously with maleficium, though the latter names harm more precisely and historically. It also intersects with demonology and demonic pact, which often provide the explanatory framework for accusations. Finally, it bears on charms, divination, and confession, where ordinary, protective, or prognostic acts may be redrawn as suspect under pastoral or polemical pressure.

Literature

Bailey, Michael D. Magic and Superstition in Europe: A Concise History from Antiquity to the Present. Lanham: Rowman & Littlefield, 2007.
Kieckhefer, Richard. Magic in the Middle Ages. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2014.
Kieckhefer, Richard. Forbidden Rites: A Necromancer's Manual of the Fifteenth Century. University Park: Pennsylvania State University Press, 1998.
Page, Sophie, and Catherine Rider, eds. The Routledge History of Medieval Magic. London: Routledge, 2019.
Rider, Catherine. Studies on pastoral and legal classification in the medieval West.

db_hits.text:
Exorcism is a Hybrid category because it names both authorized Christian rites for expelling or restraining spirits and adjacent ritual forms that could blur into illicit command, adjuration, or coercive spirit management. For MedievalMagicDB it is one of the most revealing boundary concepts in the corpus. Exorcism forces the reader to confront how close liturgical, pastoral, and magical forms could appear in language and gesture even when ecclesiastical legitimacy sharply distinguished them.

Historical Usage

In medieval usage exorcism belongs first to the sphere of authorized Christian ritual. It appears in liturgical books, pastoral practice, and sacramental or quasi-sacramental settings concerned with demonic disturbance, purification, and protection. Yet the same verbal technologies of adjuration and command could also appear in suspect rites and magical manuscripts. This does not mean the two spheres are identical. It means that historians must pay careful attention to institutional authorization, intention, textual frame, and manuscript setting.

Scholarly Significance

Exorcism matters because it shows why the history of medieval magic cannot be reduced to a simple binary between orthodoxy and deviance. Authorized ritual and illicit spirit work may share forms of address while differing radically in status and interpretation. Scholars such as Florence Chave-Mahir, Claire Fanger, and Richard Kieckhefer have helped make this borderland visible. The category is especially valuable when analyzing angelic and demonic ritual texts that borrow from or imitate authoritative liturgical styles.

Transmission and Variant Forms

Exorcistic materials circulate through service books, pastoral manuals, narrative accounts, and magical compilations. This variety means that the category has no single archive. Some exorcistic language is stable and official; some is mobile and excerpted; some is recontextualized into ritual handbooks that test the boundary of legitimacy. The study of exorcism therefore requires both liturgical history and manuscript comparison.

Related Concepts

Exorcism belongs naturally with demonology, since theories of spirit action shape ritual response. It also intersects with angelic invocation and ritual purity, because authorized and suspect rites alike often stress preparation and right relation to spiritual power. In pastoral settings it is also close to confession.

Literature

Chave-Mahir, Florence. Studies on exorcism, liturgy, and ritual authority.
Fanger, Claire. Invoking Angels: Theurgic Ideas and Practices, Thirteenth to Sixteenth Centuries. University Park: Pennsylvania State University Press, 2012.
Kieckhefer, Richard. Magic in the Middle Ages. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2014.
Rider, Catherine. Studies on pastoral and ritual boundaries in medieval religion.
Page, Sophie, and Catherine Rider, eds. The Routledge History of Medieval Magic. London: Routledge, 2019.

db_hits.text:
Hydromancy is an Actor Term for divination by water, frequently named in medieval taxonomies of forbidden or suspect arts. It is historically important not because it dominates the surviving manuscript archive, but because it repeatedly appears in lists and classifications that map the diversity of divinatory practices. In the portal it helps demonstrate how broad and differentiated the medieval vocabulary of prediction could be.

Historical Usage

Medieval writers often mention hydromancy alongside geomancy, chiromancy, pyromancy, and sortilege. These grouped arts were frequently treated as examples of illicit efforts to uncover hidden truths through unstable media. Water's reflective, moving, and receptive qualities made it a potent medium for such interpretive practice. The category therefore belongs strongly to the history of divinatory classification.

Scholarly Significance

Hydromancy matters because it shows that medieval anti-divinatory discourse regularly worked by enumeration. Even where a particular art is thinly documented in surviving manuals, its repeated appearance in taxonomic lists tells historians something about how the field of forbidden knowledge was imagined. Hydromancy is thus valuable as an entry in its own right and as part of a larger classificatory pattern.

Transmission and Variant Forms

The practice survives more strongly as a named category in condemnatory and encyclopedic listings than as a single famous text family. That makes it a good example of an art whose historical weight comes partly from classification rather than from abundant dedicated manuals. Its place in the portal is therefore relational and comparative.

Related Concepts

Hydromancy belongs with divination, geomancy, and pyromancy as part of a recurring medieval family of named arts. It also intersects with superstitio where authorities classify these practices as improper dependence on uncertain signs.

Literature

Boudet, Jean-Patrice. Studies on learned divination and occult sciences.
Rider, Catherine. Studies on pastoral and theological classification of divinatory arts.
Thorndike, Lynn. A History of Magic and Experimental Science. New York: Columbia University Press, 1923-1958.
Page, Sophie, and Catherine Rider, eds. The Routledge History of Medieval Magic. London: Routledge, 2019.

db_hits.text:
Notae is an Actor Term for the visual signs or figures most closely associated with the Ars Notoria tradition and with related modes of ritual learning. The term belongs to the medieval textual world itself rather than being a purely modern analytical label. It names diagrammatic forms that are integral to the ritual logic of certain manuscripts, not mere illustration. For that reason notae are central to any historically serious account of ritualized learning, memory, and angelic mediation in the later Middle Ages.

Historical Usage

In manuscript witnesses of the Ars Notoria and related texts, notae appear as structured visual signs linked to prayers, recitations, and ordered sequences of preparation. Medieval users did not treat them as decorative embellishment. They were part of the mechanism by which learning, understanding, and divine assistance were supposed to be pursued. The exact form and arrangement of the notae vary across manuscripts, which makes codicology and visual transmission crucial.

This historical embeddedness matters because it prevents the term from being reduced to general talk of magical diagrams. Notae belong to a specific ritual-learning environment and carry that environment with them even when reproduced or excerpted.

Scholarly Significance

Modern scholars such as Claire Fanger, Richard Kieckhefer, Frank Klaassen, and Robert Mathiesen have emphasized that the visual dimension of ritual learning cannot be reduced to words alone. Notae concentrate several key problems at once: the relation between prayer and image, the role of discipline in acquiring knowledge, and the instability of ritual books across transmission. They also resist smooth translation into purely verbal summary, which is one reason they are such an important test of good scholarly method.

Transmission and Variant Forms

The notae differ from manuscript to manuscript in form, arrangement, and accompanying explanation. Some are transmitted with more elaborate paratexts, while others are embedded in more compact ritual sequences. This variation means that the term names a family of visual instruments rather than one invariant set of figures. The study of notae is accordingly bound up with the history of editions, reproductions, and modern attempts to make these diagrams legible in print.

Because of that variability, notae are best studied as manuscript events rather than abstract symbols detached from copying practice. The portal treats them as one of the clearest cases where codicology and ritual interpretation must remain tightly linked.

Related Concepts

Notae are inseparable from Ars Notoria, which gives them their primary manuscript environment. They also belong with ritual diagrams and learned magic, though those broader categories should never erase the specifically medieval status of the term. Their devotional framing often ties them to angelic invocation and ritual purity.

Literature

Fanger, Claire. Invoking Angels: Theurgic Ideas and Practices, Thirteenth to Sixteenth Centuries. University Park: Pennsylvania State University Press, 2012.
Klaassen, Frank. The Transformations of Magic: Illicit Learned Magic in the Later Middle Ages and Renaissance. University Park: Pennsylvania State University Press, 2013.
Kieckhefer, Richard. Forbidden Rites: A Necromancer's Manual of the Fifteenth Century. University Park: Pennsylvania State University Press, 1998.
Mathiesen, Robert. Studies on the Ars Notoria and related ritual-learning traditions.
Skinner, Stephen, and Daniel Clark. Ars Notoria: The Grimoire of Rapid Learning by Magic with the Golden Flowers of Apollonius of Tyana. Modern edition and study.
